locked_facts: Fringe width: β = λD/d — fringe spacing is uniform across the screen
Bright fringe condition: d sinθ = nλ, where n = 0, ±1, ±2, …
Dark fringe condition: d sinθ = (n + ½)λ, where n = 0, ±1, ±2, …
Central bright fringe (n = 0) is always at the centre and is the brightest
Path difference between waves from two slits at point P: Δ = d sinθ ≈ dy/D (for small angles)
Intensity distribution: I = 4I₀ cos²(πd sinθ / λ), where I₀ is intensity from a single slit
If white light is used, central fringe is white but higher-order fringes show coloured edges
truth_statements: Young's experiment provided the first definitive evidence that light behaves as a wave by demonstrating interference
The fringe width β = λD/d is directly proportional to wavelength and screen distance, and inversely proportional to slit separation
All bright fringes have the same intensity in the idealised two-slit model (ignoring single-slit diffraction envelope)
The central maximum is equidistant from both slits, so the path difference is zero and constructive interference always occurs there
Coherent sources (same frequency and constant phase difference) are essential for producing a stable interference pattern

aha_moment.what_to_show: Two wavefronts emerging from the slits overlap on the screen — where crests meet crests, bright bands form; where crests meet troughs, dark bands form. A path difference counter shows exactly nλ at bright fringes and (n+½)λ at dark fringes.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Animated wavefronts from both slits reaching the screen with constructive/destructive markers and live path-difference readout
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that darkness is not absence of light but cancellation of two waves — two sources can produce less light than one at certain points

simulation_states.label: Monochromatic light hits double slit
simulation_states.description: A plane wavefront of monochromatic light approaches two narrow slits separated by distance d. The slits are labelled S₁ and S₂. No pattern is visible on the screen yet.
simulation_states.emphasis: Establish the geometry — light source, two slits, and a distant screen

simulation_states.label: Circular wavefronts from each slit
simulation_states.description: Each slit acts as a coherent secondary source (Huygens' principle). Circular wavefronts radiate outward from S₁ and S₂. Where wavefronts overlap, interference begins. No screen pattern shown yet.
simulation_states.emphasis: Both slits produce waves of the same frequency and constant phase — these are coherent sources

simulation_states.label: Interference pattern forms on screen
simulation_states.description: The screen lights up with alternating bright and dark bands. The central bright fringe is marked n = 0. Path difference readout shows Δ = 0 at centre, Δ = λ at first bright fringe, Δ = λ/2 at first dark fringe. Formula β = λD/d displayed.
simulation_states.emphasis: Bright bands where path difference = nλ (constructive), dark bands where path difference = (n+½)λ (destructive)

simulation_states.label: Interactive exploration
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts wavelength λ, slit separation d, and screen distance D. Fringe pattern updates in real time — wider fringes for longer λ or larger D, narrower fringes for larger d. Numerical fringe width β displayed.
simulation_states.emphasis: Explore how each parameter affects fringe width — verify β = λD/d experimentally

common_misconceptions.belief: The central fringe is dark because the two waves cancel each other
common_misconceptions.correction: The central fringe is always bright because the path difference from both slits to the centre is zero, so the waves always arrive in phase and interfere constructively
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Highlight path difference = 0 at centre with both wavefronts arriving crest-to-crest

common_misconceptions.belief: Increasing slit separation makes the fringes wider
trigger_phrases: slits zyada door toh fringe bada
common_misconceptions.correction: Fringe width β = λD/d — increasing slit separation d actually makes fringes narrower, not wider. The relationship is inversely proportional.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Animate d increasing while showing fringes compressing together with numerical β decreasing

common_misconceptions.belief: Fringes disappear if the light source is not a laser
common_misconceptions.correction: Any coherent light source works. Young originally used sunlight through a single slit to create spatial coherence. The key requirement is coherence (constant phase relationship), not specifically a laser.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show that a single slit before the double slit creates coherent illumination even from an extended source
